    return u0_vals[:S] - sol[:S][end]
end

# Generate final size distribution
n_sims = 1000
final_sizes = [final_size(u0, params, (0.0, 50.0)) for _ in 1:n_sims]

# Plot histogram
histogram(final_sizes, bins=30, normalize=:pdf, alpha=0.7,
          title="Final Size Distribution (N=$N, R₀=$(λ/γ))",
          xlabel="Final Size", ylabel="Probability Density")

# Calculate empirical statistics
mean_final = mean(final_sizes)
std_final = std(final_sizes)
println("Mean final size: $(round(mean_final, digits=1))")
println("Standard deviation: $(round(std_final, digits=1))")

# Estimate probability of minor epidemic (final size < 5% of population)
minor_threshold = 0.05 * N
p_minor_empirical = count(z -> z < minor_threshold, final_sizes) / n_sims
p_minor_theoretical = minor_epidemic_probability(λ/γ)

println("Empirical P(minor epidemic): $(round(p_minor_empirical, digits=4))")
println("Theoretical P(minor epidemic): $(round(p_minor_theoretical, digits=4))")

2.16 4.6 Tau-Leaping for Approximate Fast Simulation

2.16.1 4.6.1 The Tau-Leaping Method

For systems with high reaction rates, the Gillespie algorithm becomes computationally expensive due to many small time steps. Tau-leaping approximates the process by assuming rates remain constant over small intervals τ.

Algorithm 4.2 (Tau-Leaping Method). 1. Choose time step τ small enough that rates don’t change significantly 2. For each reaction channel j with rate rⱼ, generate number of firings: Kⱼ ~ Poisson(rⱼτ) 3. Update state by applying all reactions: X(t+τ) = X(t) + Σⱼ Kⱼνⱼ 4. Increment time: t ← t + τ

The challenge lies in choosing τ adaptively to balance accuracy and efficiency.

2.16.2 4.6.2 Adaptive Tau Selection

A common strategy ensures that each rate changes by at most a fraction ε:

τ ≤ min(ε|Xᵢ|/|aᵢ|, ε²σᵢ²/bᵢ²)

where aᵢ and bᵢ are the mean and standard deviation of the change in species i per unit time.

using Random, Distributions, Plots

function tau_leaping_sir(S0, I0, R0, λ, γ, N, T_final; ε=0.03)
    # Initialize
    S, I, R = S0, I0, R0
    t = 0.0
    
    # Storage for trajectory
    times = [t]
    S_traj = [S]
    I_traj = [I] 
    R_traj = [R]
    
    while t < T_final && I > 0
        # Calculate reaction rates
        r1 = λ * S * I / N  # Infection rate
        r2 = γ * I          # Recovery rate
        
        # Adaptive tau selection
        if I > 0
            # Mean changes per unit time
            a_S = -r1
            a_I = r1 - r2
            
            # Standard deviations of changes per unit time  
            b_S = sqrt(r1)
            b_I = sqrt(r1 + r2)
            
            # Tau selection (avoid division by zero)
            tau_candidates = Float64[]
            
            if abs(a_S) > 0 && S > 0
                push!(tau_candidates, ε * S / abs(a_S))
            end
            if abs(a_I) > 0 && I > 0  
                push!(tau_candidates, ε * I / abs(a_I))
            end
            if b_S > 0 && S > 0
                push!(tau_candidates, (ε * S)^2 / b_S^2) 
            end
            if b_I > 0 && I > 0
                push!(tau_candidates, (ε * I)^2 / b_I^2)
            end
            
            if isempty(tau_candidates)
                break
            end
            
            τ = min(minimum(tau_candidates), T_final - t, 0.1)
        else
            break
        end
        
        # Generate number of reactions in interval [t, t+τ)
        K1 = rand(Poisson(r1 * τ))  # Infections
        K2 = rand(Poisson(r2 * τ))  # Recoveries
        
        # Update state (ensure non-negative values)
        S = max(0, S - K1)
        I = max(0, I + K1 - K2)
        R = min(N, R + K2)
        
        # Store trajectory point
        t += τ
        push!(times, t)
        push!(S_traj, S)
        push!(I_traj, I)
        push!(R_traj, R)
    end
    
    return (times, S_traj, I_traj, R_traj)
end

# Compare tau-leaping with exact Gillespie
Random.seed!(123)

# Parameters
N = 5000
λ = 1.2
γ = 0.5
S0, I0, R0 = 4950, 50, 0
T_final = 15.0

# Tau-leaping simulation
t_tau, S_tau, I_tau, R_tau = tau_leaping_sir(S0, I0, R0, λ, γ, N, T_final)

# Exact Gillespie for comparison
Random.seed!(123)
u0_exact = [:S => S0, :I => I0, :R => R0]
params_exact = [:λ => λ, :γ => γ, :N => N]
dprob = DiscreteProblem(sir_model, u0_exact, (0.0, T_final), params_exact)
jprob = JumpProblem(sir_model, dprob, Direct())
sol_exact = solve(jprob, SSAStepper(), saveat=0.05)

# Plot comparison
plt = plot(title="Tau-Leaping vs Exact Gillespie", xlabel="Time", ylabel="Number")
plot!(plt, t_tau, I_tau, label="Tau-leaping", linewidth=2, color=:red)
plot!(plt, sol_exact.t, sol_exact[:I], label="Exact Gillespie", 
      linewidth=2, color=:blue, linestyle=:dash)

display(plt)

# Compute timing comparison
using BenchmarkTools

println("Tau-leaping performance:")
@btime tau_leaping_sir($S0, $I0, $R0, $λ, $γ, $N, $T_final)

println("\nGillespie performance:")
@btime solve($jprob, SSAStepper())

2.17 4.7 Branching Process Approximation for Early Epidemic

2.17.1 4.7.1 Galton-Watson Process

During the initial phase of an epidemic when the number of infected individuals is small and the population is largely susceptible, each infected individual acts independently, generating offspring according to a reproduction distribution.

Definition 4.5 (Epidemic Branching Process). Let Zₙ denote the number of infected individuals in generation n. The process {Zₙ} follows a Galton-Watson branching process where each individual in generation n produces a random number of offspring in generation n+1 according to distribution {pₖ}.

For the SIR model, each infected individual has an infectious period exponentially distributed with rate γ, during which it makes contacts at rate λ. In a fully susceptible population, the number of secondary infections follows a Poisson distribution with mean R₀ = λ/γ.

2.17.2 4.7.2 Probability Generating Functions

Theorem 4.5 (Extinction Probability). Let G(s) = Σₖ pₖsᵏ be the probability generating function of the offspring distribution. The extinction probability ρ satisfies ρ = G(ρ), and for supercritical processes (mean offspring > 1), this gives the smallest positive root.

For Poisson offspring with mean R₀: G(s) = e^(-R₀(1-s))

The extinction probability satisfies ρ = e^(-R₀(1-ρ)), consistent with our earlier result.

2.17.3 4.7.3 Generation Time Distribution

Real epidemics don’t occur in discrete generations. We need to account for the continuous-time nature of transmission and recovery.

Definition 4.6 (Generation Time). The generation time Tᵨ is the time between infection of a primary case and infection of a secondary case it generates.

For the SIR model, the generation time has probability density: f(τ) = λe^(-(λ+γ)τ)

This represents the probability that transmission occurs at time τ, given the individual remains infectious until at least time τ.

2.17.4 4.7.4 Computational Implementation

using Distributions, Plots, Random

# Simulate branching process approximation for early epidemic
function simulate_branching_epidemic(R0, max_generations=20, max_total=10000)
    generations = [1]  # Start with 1 infected individual
    total_infected = 1
    
    for gen in 1:max_generations
        current_gen_size = generations[end]
        if current_gen_size == 0 || total_infected > max_total
            break
        end
        
        # Each individual produces Poisson(R0) offspring
        next_gen_size = sum(rand(Poisson(R0)) for _ in 1:current_gen_size)
        push!(generations, next_gen_size)
        total_infected += next_gen_size
    end
    
    return generations, total_infected
end

# Function to estimate extinction probability empirically
function estimate_extinction_probability(R0, n_sims=10000)
    extinctions = 0
    
    for _ in 1:n_sims
        gens, total = simulate_branching_epidemic(R0, 50, 1000)
        if total < 100  # Consider epidemic "extinct" if <100 total cases
            extinctions += 1
        end
    end
    
    return extinctions / n_sims
end

# Compare theoretical and empirical extinction probabilities
R0_vals = [0.8, 1.2, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0]
theoretical_probs = [R0 <= 1 ? 1.0 : minor_epidemic_probability(R0) for R0 in R0_vals]

Random.seed!(42)
empirical_probs = [estimate_extinction_probability(R0) for R0 in R0_vals]

# Plot comparison
plt = plot(R0_vals, theoretical_probs, marker=:circle, linewidth=2,
          label="Theoretical", xlabel="R₀", ylabel="Extinction Probability",
          title="Branching Process Extinction Probability")
plot!(plt, R0_vals, empirical_probs, marker=:square, linewidth=2, 
      label="Empirical (branching)", linestyle=:dash)

display(plt)

# Print comparison
println("R₀\tTheoretical\tEmpirical")
for (i, R0) in enumerate(R0_vals)
    println("$(R0)\t$(round(theoretical_probs[i], digits=4))\t\t$(round(empirical_probs[i], digits=4))")
end

2.17.5 4.7.5 Early Growth Phase Analysis

The branching process also provides insights into the initial exponential growth rate.

Theorem 4.6 (Malthusian Parameter). For a supercritical branching process in continuous time, the population grows exponentially at rate r, where r is the unique positive solution to:

∫₀^∞ e^(-rt) λe^(-(λ+γ)t) dt = 1

For the SIR model, this gives: λ/(λ + γ + r) = 1

Solving for r yields r = λ - γ = γ(R₀ - 1), which matches the initial growth rate from the deterministic analysis in Chapter 1.

2.18 4.8 Exercises

Exercise 4.1 (Computational). Implement the master equation for a stochastic SIS model (no recovered class) with N = 50 individuals. Use matrix exponentiation to compute the probability distribution at time t = 5 for parameters λ =

3 Chapter 5: Age-Structured and Multi-Group Models

The classical SIR model assumes a homogeneously mixing population where all individuals have identical rates of contact and susceptibility. However, real populations exhibit significant heterogeneity in contact patterns, disease susceptibility, and recovery rates across different demographic groups, particularly age classes. Children attend schools with high contact rates, adults participate in workplace networks, and elderly populations often have reduced social contacts but increased disease severity. This chapter develops the mathematical framework for incorporating population structure into epidemic models, focusing on age-structured formulations and multi-group extensions.

3.1 5.1 Age-Structured Epidemic Models

3.1.1 5.1.1 The McKendrick-von Foerster Framework

Age-structured population dynamics are naturally described by partial differential equations where individuals are characterized by both time t and age a. For infectious disease transmission, we extend the McKendrick-von Foerster equation to include infection dynamics.

Definition 5.1 (Age-Structured SIR System). Let S(a,t), I(a,t), and R(a,t) denote the densities of susceptible, infected, and recovered individuals of age a at time t. The age-structured SIR system is given by:

∂S/∂t + ∂S/∂a = -λ(a,t)S(a,t) - μ(a)S(a,t)

∂I/∂t + ∂I/∂a = λ(a,t)S(a,t) - γ(a)I(a,t) - μ(a)I(a,t)

∂R/∂t + ∂R/∂a = γ(a)I(a,t) - μ(a)R(a,t)

where λ(a,t) is the age-specific force of infection, γ(a) is the age-specific recovery rate, and μ(a) is the age-specific mortality rate.

The force of infection incorporates age-dependent mixing patterns through the contact structure:

λ(a,t) = ∫₀^∞ β(a,a’)c(a,a’)I(a’,t) da’

where β(a,a’) represents the transmission probability per contact between individuals of ages a and a’, and c(a,a’) describes the contact rate between age groups.

The boundary condition represents new births:

S(0,t) = ∫₀^∞ b(a)N(a,t) da I(0,t) = 0 R(0,t) = 0

where b(a) is the age-specific birth rate and N(a,t) = S(a,t) + I(a,t) + R(a,t).

3.1.2 5.1.2 Steady-State Age Distribution

In the absence of disease (I ≡ 0), the population reaches a steady-state age distribution determined by the demographic parameters. The stable age distribution satisfies:

Theorem 5.1 (Stable Age Distribution). Under constant demographic rates, the stable age distribution is given by:

N*(a) = N₀b₀ exp(-∫₀^a μ(s) ds)

where b₀ is the crude birth rate and N₀ is the total population size.

Proof. At steady state, ∂N/∂t = 0, so the equation becomes:

dN/da = -μ(a)N(a)

with boundary condition N(0) = B = ∫₀^∞ b(a)N(a) da. Solving the differential equation:

N(a) = N(0) exp(-∫₀^a μ(s) ds)

The total population constraint ∫₀^∞ N(a) da = N₀ determines N(0). □

3.1.3 5.1.3 Age-Dependent Basic Reproduction Number

The basic reproduction number for age-structured models involves the next-generation operator approach. Define the age-at-infection distribution φ(a) for a completely susceptible population.

Definition 5.2 (Age-Structured R₀). The basic reproduction number is the dominant eigenvalue of the next-generation operator:

R₀ = ∫₀^∞ ∫₀^∞ K(a,a’) φ(a’) da’ da

where K(a,a’) = β(a,a’)c(a,a’)S*(a)/γ(a) is the expected number of secondary infections in age class a caused by an infected individual of age a’.

3.2 5.2 WAIFW Matrix and Contact Patterns

3.2.1 5.2.1 The Who Acquires Infection From Whom Matrix

For practical implementation, age is discretized into classes leading to matrix formulations. Let there be n age classes with population sizes Nᵢ.

Definition 5.3 (WAIFW Matrix). The “Who Acquires Infection From Whom” matrix W has elements wᵢⱼ representing the rate at which susceptibles in age group i acquire infection from infected individuals in age group j:

wᵢⱼ = βᵢⱼcᵢⱼ

where βᵢⱼ is the transmission probability per contact and cᵢⱼ is the contact rate between groups i and j.

The discrete age-structured SIR system becomes:

dSᵢ/dt = -Sᵢ ∑ⱼ wᵢⱼ Iⱼ/Nⱼ - μᵢSᵢ + bᵢN

dIᵢ/dt = Sᵢ ∑ⱼ wᵢⱼ Iⱼ/Nⱼ - (γᵢ + μᵢ)Iᵢ

dRᵢ/dt = γᵢIᵢ - μᵢRᵢ

3.2.2 5.2.2 Social Contact Matrices

Contact patterns are highly age-assortative, with strong within-age-group mixing. The POLYMOD study provided empirical contact matrices for European populations.

Definition 5.4 (Social Contact Matrix). The social contact matrix C has elements cᵢⱼ representing the average number of contacts per day between individuals in age group i and age group j.

Key properties of contact matrices: - Reciprocity: Nᵢcᵢⱼ = Nⱼcⱼᵢ (total contacts must balance) - Age-assortativity: Diagonal elements typically dominate - Workplace effects: Strong mixing in adult age classes - School effects: High mixing in child/adolescent classes

using LinearAlgebra, Plots, DifferentialEquations

# POLYMOD-inspired contact matrix for 5 age groups
# Age groups: 0-4, 5-19, 20-39, 40-59, 60+
function polymod_contact_matrix()
    C = [
        7.64  3.13  1.80  0.79  0.30;
        1.82  14.81 2.53  1.83  0.54;
        1.65  4.15  8.32  2.87  0.83;
        0.53  2.27  3.65  5.42  1.23;
        0.60  1.49  2.33  2.26  2.88
    ]
    return C
end

# Population sizes (millions) for age groups
N_ages = [3.2, 12.1, 16.8, 13.2, 8.7]  # Example demographics

# Ensure reciprocity: N_i * c_ij = N_j * c_ji
function enforce_reciprocity(C, N)
    n = length(N)
    C_reciprocal = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            if i <= j
                C_reciprocal[i,j] = C[i,j]
                C_reciprocal[j,i] = (N[i] / N[j]) * C[i,j]
            end
        end
    end
    return C_reciprocal
end

C = polymod_contact_matrix()
C_recip = enforce_reciprocity(C, N_ages)

println("Contact matrix (reciprocal):")
display(round.(C_recip, digits=2))

3.2.3 5.2.3 Transmission Parameters

The WAIFW matrix combines contact patterns with age-specific transmission probabilities:

# Age-specific transmission probabilities (per contact)
β_ages = [0.05, 0.04, 0.03, 0.035, 0.06]  # Higher for young and old

# Age-specific recovery rates (1/infectious period in days)
γ_ages = [1/5, 1/6, 1/7, 1/7, 1/10]  # Slower recovery in elderly

# Age-specific mortality rates (per year)
μ_ages = [0.001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.003, 0.02]

# Construct WAIFW matrix
function waifw_matrix(β, C, N)
    n = length(β)
    W = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            W[i,j] = β[j] * C[i,j]  # β_j: infectiousness of age j
        end
    end
    return W
end

W = waifw_matrix(β_ages, C_recip, N_ages)
println("WAIFW matrix:")
display(round.(W, digits=4))

3.3 5.3 Multi-Group Models and Heterogeneous Mixing

3.3.1 5.3.1 General Multi-Group Framework

Beyond age, populations can be stratified by risk behavior, geographic location, occupation, or other characteristics. The multi-group framework provides a unified approach.

Definition 5.5 (Multi-Group SIR Model). For n population groups with sizes N₁, …, Nₙ, the multi-group SIR system is:

dSᵢ/dt = -Sᵢ ∑ⱼ₌₁ⁿ βᵢⱼ cᵢⱼ Iⱼ/Nⱼ + δᵢN - μᵢSᵢ

dIᵢ/dt = Sᵢ ∑ⱼ₌₁ⁿ βᵢⱼ cᵢⱼ Iⱼ/Nⱼ - (γᵢ + μᵢ)Iᵢ

dRᵢ/dt = γᵢIᵢ - μᵢRᵢ

where δᵢ represents the birth/recruitment rate into group i.

3.3.2 5.3.2 Proportionate and Preferred Mixing

Contact patterns can be modeled as combinations of random (proportionate) and assortative (preferred) mixing.

Definition 5.6 (Mixing Patterns). The contact rate between groups i and j can be written as:

cᵢⱼ = (1-ε)c̄ᵢ (Nⱼ/N) + ε cᵢⱼᵖʳᵉᶠ

where ε ∈ [0,1] measures the degree of assortativity, c̄ᵢ is the total contact rate for group i, and cᵢⱼᵖʳᵉᶠ represents preferred mixing patterns.

For proportionate mixing (ε = 0), contacts are distributed according to group sizes. For complete assortativity (ε = 1), groups mix only with themselves.

3.3.3 5.3.3 Basic Reproduction Number for Multi-Group Models

The next-generation matrix approach extends naturally to multi-group settings.

Theorem 5.2 (Multi-Group R₀). For a multi-group model, R₀ is the spectral radius of the next-generation matrix:

K = FV⁻¹

where F is the transmission matrix with elements Fᵢⱼ = βᵢⱼ cᵢⱼ Sᵢ*/Nⱼ and V is the transition matrix with diagonal elements Vᵢᵢ = γᵢ + μᵢ.

Proof. The linearization of the infection subsystem near the disease-free equilibrium yields ẋ = (F - V)x where x represents the infected compartments. The condition for invasion is that F - V has positive dominant eigenvalue, equivalent to ρ(FV⁻¹) > 1. □

# Calculate R0 for multi-group model
function calculate_R0_multigroup(W, γ, N_total)
    n = length(γ)
    
    # Next-generation matrix K = F * V^(-1)
    # F[i,j] = transmission rate from j to i
    # V[i,i] = removal rate from i
    
    F = zeros(n, n)
    V = diagm(γ)  # Assuming no vital dynamics for simplicity
    
    for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            F[i,j] = W[i,j]  # W already incorporates contact patterns
        end
    end
    
    K = F * inv(V)
    R0 = maximum(real.(eigvals(K)))
    
    return R0, K
end

R0, K = calculate_R0_multigroup(W, γ_ages, sum(N_ages))
println("Multi-group R₀ = $(round(R0, digits=3))")
println("Next-generation matrix eigenvalues:")
println(round.(real.(eigvals(K)), digits=3))

3.4 5.4 Targeted Vaccination Strategies

3.4.1 5.4.1 Optimal Vaccination Allocation

With limited vaccine supplies, the question becomes how to optimally allocate vaccines across age groups to minimize epidemic impact.

Definition 5.7 (Vaccination Impact Matrix). Define the vaccination impact matrix V with elements vᵢⱼ representing the reduction in infections in age group i per vaccine administered to age group j:

vᵢⱼ = ∂I*ᵢ/∂pⱼ

where I*ᵢ is the final epidemic size in group i and pⱼ is the vaccination coverage in group j.

For small vaccination coverages, the impact is approximated by the dominant eigenvector of the next-generation matrix.

Theorem 5.3 (Optimal Vaccination Strategy). Under budget constraint ∑ᵢ pᵢNᵢ = V_total, the vaccination strategy that minimizes total infections allocates vaccines proportionally to the dominant eigenvector components weighted by group sizes.

# Optimal vaccination allocation
function optimal_vaccination(K, N, vaccine_total)
    # Dominant eigenvector gives vaccination priorities
    λ_max, v_max = eigen(K)
    dominant_idx = argmax(real.(λ_max))
    priorities = real.(v_max[:, dominant_idx])
    
    # Normalize priorities to be positive
    priorities = abs.(priorities)
    
    # Allocate proportionally to priorities * group sizes
    allocation_weights = priorities .* N
    allocation_weights ./= sum(allocation_weights)
    
    vaccination_allocation = vaccine_total * allocation_weights
    coverage_by_group = vaccination_allocation ./ N
    
    return coverage_by_group, vaccination_allocation
end

vaccine_supply = 5.0e6  # 5 million doses
coverage, allocation = optimal_vaccination(K, N_ages, vaccine_supply)

println("Optimal vaccination strategy:")
for i in 1:length(N_ages)
    println("Age group $i: $(round(allocation[i]/1e6, digits=2))M doses ($(round(100*coverage[i], digits=1))% coverage)")
end

3.4.2 5.4.2 Ring Vaccination and Contact Tracing

For diseases with longer infectious periods, targeted vaccination of contacts becomes feasible.

# Ring vaccination model with contact tracing
function ring_vaccination_model(contact_matrix, trace_prob, vaccine_efficacy)
    n = size(contact_matrix, 1)
    
    # Effective contact matrix accounting for tracing and vaccination
    C_eff = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            # Probability that contact i-j leads to transmission
            # (1 - trace_prob * vaccine_efficacy) accounts for successful
            # identification and vaccination of contact
            reduction_factor = 1 - trace_prob * vaccine_efficacy
            C_eff[i,j] = contact_matrix[i,j] * reduction_factor
        end
    end
    
    return C_eff
end

# Example: 70% contact tracing efficiency, 90% vaccine efficacy
C_traced = ring_vaccination_model(C_recip, 0.7, 0.9)
W_traced = waifw_matrix(β_ages, C_traced, N_ages)
R0_traced, _ = calculate_R0_multigroup(W_traced, γ_ages, sum(N_ages))

println("R₀ with ring vaccination: $(round(R0_traced, digits=3))")
println("Reduction: $(round(100*(1-R0_traced/R0), digits=1))%")

3.5 5.5 Age-Structured Model Implementation

3.5.1 5.5.1 Numerical Solution of Age-Structured PDEs

For the continuous age-structured model, we can use method of characteristics or finite difference schemes.

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

# Discretized age-structured SIR model
function age_structured_sir!(du, u, p, t)
    β, γ, μ, C, N_birth = p
    n_ages = length(γ)
    
    # Extract state variables
    S = u[1:n_ages]
    I = u[n_ages+1:2*n_ages]
    R = u[2*n_ages+1:3*n_ages]
    N = S + I + R
    
    # Force of infection for each age group
    λ = zeros(n_ages)
    for i in 1:n_ages
        for j in 1:n_ages
            λ[i] += β[j] * C[i,j] * I[j] / N[j]
        end
    end
    
    # Derivatives
    dS = @view du[1:n_ages]
    dI = @view du[n_ages+1:2*n_ages]
    dR = @view du[2*n_ages+1:3*n_ages]
    
    for i in 1:n_ages
        dS[i] = -λ[i] * S[i] - μ[i] * S[i]
        dI[i] = λ[i] * S[i] - (γ[i] + μ[i]) * I[i]
        dR[i] = γ[i] * I[i] - μ[i] * R[i]
    end
    
    # Add births to first age group
    total_births = sum(N_birth .* N)
    dS[1] += total_births
end

# Initial conditions: mostly susceptible with small infection in age group 2
n_ages = 5
S0 = N_ages .* 0.99
I0 = zeros(n_ages)
I0[2] = N_ages[2] * 0.01  # Introduce infection in school-age children
R0 = zeros(n_ages)

u0 = vcat(S0, I0, R0)

# Birth rates (simplified)
birth_rates = [0.02, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0]  # Only births into age group 1

# Parameters
params = (β_ages, γ_ages, μ_ages/365, C_recip, birth_rates/365)

# Solve ODE
tspan = (0.0, 365.0)  # One year
prob = ODEProblem(age_structured_sir!, u0, tspan, params)
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=1.0)

# Extract and plot results
times = sol.t
S_sol = sol[1:n_ages, :]
I_sol = sol[n_ages+1:2*n_ages, :]

# Plot infection dynamics by age group
p1 = plot(times, I_sol', 
          labels=["0-4" "5-19" "20-39" "40-59" "60+"],
          xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Infected individuals",
          title="Age-Structured Epidemic Dynamics",
          linewidth=2)

display(p1)

3.5.2 5.5.2 Epidemic Final Size by Age

The final size relation extends to age-structured models through the implicit equation system.

Theorem 5.4 (Age-Structured Final Size). The final sizes z₁, …, zₙ for each age group satisfy:

zᵢ = 1 - exp(-∑ⱼ wᵢⱼ zⱼ/Nⱼ)

where wᵢⱼ are the elements of the WAIFW matrix.

# Calculate final epidemic size by age group
function final_size_age_structured(W, N; max_iter=1000, tol=1e-8)
    n = length(N)
    z = fill(0.5, n)  # Initial guess
    
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        z_new = zeros(n)
        
        for i in 1:n
            force_sum = sum(W[i,j] * z[j] / N[j] for j in 1:n)
            z_new[i] = 1 - exp(-force_sum)
        end
        
        if maximum(abs.(z_new - z)) < tol
            return z_new
        end
        
        z = z_new
    end
    
    @warn "Final size calculation did not converge"
    return z
end

final_sizes = final_size_age_structured(W, N_ages)
attack_rates = final_sizes .* 100

println("Final attack rates by age group:")
age_labels = ["0-4", "5-19", "20-39", "40-59", "60+"]
for i in 1:length(age_labels)
    println("$(age_labels[i]): $(round(attack_rates[i], digits=1))%")
end

3.6 5.6 Pair-Formation Models for STIs

3.6.1 5.6.1 Partnership Formation and Dissolution

Sexually transmitted infections require explicit modeling of sexual partnerships, leading to pair-formation models.

Definition 5.8 (Pair-Formation Model). Let X, Y represent single males and females, and Z represent partnerships. The dynamics include:

Partnership formation: X + Y → Z (rate ρ)
Partnership dissolution: Z → X + Y (rate σ)
Disease transmission: Within partnerships at rate τ per partnership per unit time

The system becomes:

dX/dt = σZ - ρXY + γ₁I₁ - μX
dY/dt = σZ - ρXY + γ₂I₂ - μY
dZ/dt = ρXY - σZ - (infection rates)

3.6.2 5.6.2 Transmission Within Partnerships

# Simplified pair-formation STI model
function sti_pair_model!(du, u, p, t)
    ρ, σ, τ, γ, μ = p
    
    # Single susceptible males/females
    X_s, Y_s = u[1], u[2]
    # Single infected males/females  
    X_i, Y_i = u[3], u[4]
    # Partnerships: SS, SI, IS, II
    Z_ss, Z_si, Z_is, Z_ii = u[5], u[6], u[7], u[8]
    
    # Total singles
    X_total = X_s + X_i
    Y_total = Y_s + Y_i
    
    # Formation rates (proportional to composition)
    form_ss = ρ * X_s * Y_s
    form_si = ρ * X_s * Y_i  
    form_is = ρ * X_i * Y_s
    form_ii = ρ * X_i * Y_i
    
    # Dissolution rates
    diss_ss = σ * Z_ss
    diss_si = σ * Z_si
    diss_is = σ * Z_is  
    diss_ii = σ * Z_ii
    
    # Transmission within partnerships
    si_to_ii = τ * Z_si  # S→I transmission to male
    is_to_ii = τ * Z_is  # S→I transmission to female
    
    # Single susceptible males
    du[1] = diss_ss + diss_si - form_ss - form_si - μ*X_s
    
    # Single susceptible females
    du[2] = diss_ss + diss_is - form_ss - form_is - μ*Y_s
    
    # Single infected males
    du[3] = diss_is + diss_ii - form_is - form_ii + γ*X_i - μ*X_i
    
    # Single infected females  
    du[4] = diss_si + diss_ii - form_si - form_ii + γ*Y_i - μ*Y_i
    
    # SS partnerships
    du[5] = form_ss - diss_ss
    
    # SI partnerships (male infected)
    du[6] = form_si - diss_si - si_to_ii
    
    # IS partnerships (female infected)
    du[7] = form_is - diss_is - is_to_ii
    
    # II partnerships
    du[8] = form_ii - diss_ii + si_to_ii + is_to_ii
end

# Example parameters
ρ = 0.1    # Formation rate
σ = 0.05   # Dissolution rate  
τ = 0.3    # Transmission rate per partnership
γ = 0.1    # Recovery rate
μ = 0.01   # Death/birth rate

# Initial conditions: mostly susceptible singles
u0_sti = [100.0, 100.0, 5.0, 5.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0]

params_sti = (ρ, σ, τ, γ, μ)
tspan_sti = (0.0, 100.0)

prob_sti = ODEProblem(sti_pair_model!, u0_sti, tspan_sti, params_sti)
sol_sti = solve(prob_sti, Tsit5(), saveat=0.5)

# Plot STI dynamics
p_sti = plot(sol_sti.t, sol_sti[3,:] + sol_sti[4,:], 
             label="Total Infected Singles",
             xlabel="Time", ylabel="Individuals",
             title="STI Pair-Formation Model")
plot!(p_sti, sol_sti.t, sol_sti[6,:] + sol_sti[7,:] + 2*sol_sti[8,:], 
      label="Infected in Partnerships")

display(p_sti)

3.7 5.7 Seasonal Forcing and School Terms

3.7.1 5.7.1 Time-Varying Contact Rates

Contact patterns vary seasonally, particularly for school-age children. This leads to periodic forcing in the transmission parameters.

# Seasonal contact matrix with school effects
function seasonal_contacts(t, C_base, school_multiplier=2.0, school_ages=[2])
    C_seasonal = copy(C_base)
    
    # School term effect (simplified sinusoidal)
    school_factor = 1 + 0.3 * sin(2π * t / 365)  # Varies ±30% annually
    
    for i in school_ages, j in school_ages
        C_seasonal[i,j] *= school_factor * school_multiplier
    end
    
    return C_seasonal
end

# Age-structured model with seasonal forcing
function seasonal_age_sir!(du, u, p, t)
    β, γ, μ, C_base, N_birth = p
    n_ages = length(γ)
    
    # Get time-varying contact matrix
    C_t = seasonal_contacts(t, C_base)
    
    S = u[1:n_ages]
    I = u[n_ages+1:2*n_ages]  
    R = u[2*n_ages+1:3*n_ages]
    N = S + I + R
    
    # Force of infection with seasonal contacts
    λ = zeros(n_ages)
    for i in 1:n_ages
        for j in 1:n_ages
            λ[i] += β[j] * C_t[i,j] * I[j] / N[j]
        end
    end
    
    dS = @view du[1:n_ages]
    dI = @view du[n_ages+1:2*n_ages]
    dR = @view du[2*n_ages+1:3*n_ages]
    
    for i in 1:n_ages
        dS[i] = -λ[i] * S[i] - μ[i] * S[i]
        dI[i] = λ[i] * S[i] - (γ[i] + μ[i]) * I[i]  
        dR[i] = γ[i] * I[i] - μ[i] * R[i]
    end
    
    # Births
    dS[1] += sum(N_birth .* N)
end

# Solve seasonal model for multiple years
params_seasonal = (β_ages, γ_ages, μ_ages/365, C_recip, birth_rates/365)
tspan_seasonal = (0.0, 3*365.0)  # 3 years
prob_seasonal = ODEProblem(seasonal_age_sir!, u0, tspan_seasonal, params_seasonal)
sol_seasonal = solve(prob_seasonal, Tsit5(), saveat=7.0)  # Weekly output

# Plot seasonal epidemic waves
times_years = sol_seasonal.t / 365
I_total = sum(sol_seasonal[n_ages+1:2*n_ages, :], dims=1)[:]

p_seasonal = plot(times_years, I_total,
                 xlabel="Time (years)", ylabel="Total Infected",
                 title="Seasonal Age-Structured Epidemics",
                 linewidth=2)

display(p_seasonal)

3.8 5.8 Exercises

Exercise 5.1 (Theoretical). Prove that the age-structured model admits a unique disease-free equilibrium when all infected compartments are zero. Show that this equilibrium has the stable age distribution given in Theorem 5.1.

Exercise 5.2 (Computational). Implement the continuous age-structured SIR model using a finite difference discretization in age. Compare your results with the discrete age-group approach using n = 5, 10, 20 age classes. How does the resolution affect the epidemic dynamics?

# Starter code for Exercise 5.2
function continuous_age_sir(n_ages, max_age=80.0)
    # Discretize age from 0 to max_age with n_ages points
    # Implement finite difference scheme for ∂/∂a terms
    # Return solution as function of time and age
end

Exercise 5.3 (Theoretical). For a two-group model with groups of sizes N₁, N₂ and WAIFW matrix elements w₁

4 Chapter 6: Spatial Epidemic Models

The classical compartmental models considered in Chapters 1-5 assume well-mixed populations where every individual has equal contact probability with every other individual. In reality, infectious diseases spread through populations with complex spatial structure. Individuals interact preferentially with nearby neighbors, travel creates long-range connections, and geographic barriers influence transmission patterns. This chapter develops mathematical frameworks for understanding how spatial structure affects epidemic dynamics, from continuous reaction-diffusion models to discrete network-based approaches.

4.1 6.1 Reaction-Diffusion SIR Models

The most direct extension of the SIR model to spatial dynamics incorporates diffusion terms representing random movement of individuals. Consider a one-dimensional spatial domain where susceptible, infected, and recovered individuals can move according to simple diffusion processes.

4.1.1 6.1.1 The Spatial SIR System

Let S(x,t), I(x,t), and R(x,t) denote the densities of susceptible, infected, and recovered individuals at position x and time t. The reaction-diffusion SIR system is:

∂S/∂t = D_S ∂²S/∂x² - βSI
∂I/∂t = D_I ∂²I/∂x² + βSI - γI  
∂R/∂t = D_R ∂²R/∂x² + γI

where D_S, D_I, D_R are diffusion coefficients and β, γ are the transmission and recovery rates from the classical SIR model.

Definition 6.1 (Spatial Basic Reproduction Number). For the reaction-diffusion SIR system, the spatial basic reproduction number is R₀ = β N₀/γ, where N₀ = S₀ + I₀ + R₀ is the total initial population density.

The key difference from the non-spatial case is that local depletion of susceptibles can create spatial heterogeneity in transmission potential.

4.1.2 6.1.2 Traveling Wave Solutions

When an epidemic spreads spatially, it often takes the form of a traveling wave. We seek solutions of the form S(x,t) = S(z), I(x,t) = I(z), R(x,t) = R(z) where z = x - ct is the traveling wave coordinate with wave speed c.

Theorem 6.1 (Existence of Traveling Waves). For the reaction-diffusion SIR system with equal diffusion coefficients D_S = D_I = D_R = D, there exists a critical wave speed c* such that: 1. For c ≥ c, there exist traveling wave solutions connecting the disease-free state ahead of the wave to an endemic state behind the wave. 2. For c < c, no such traveling wave solutions exist.

Proof Sketch. Transform to the traveling wave coordinate z = x - ct. The system becomes:

-c S'(z) = D S''(z) - βS(z)I(z)
-c I'(z) = D I''(z) + βS(z)I(z) - γI(z)
-c R'(z) = D R''(z) + γI(z)

The boundary conditions are S(-∞) = S₀, I(-∞) = 0, R(-∞) = 0 (disease-free ahead of wave) and S(∞) = S_e, I(∞) = I_e, R(∞) = R_e (endemic behind wave).

For the leading edge where I is small, linearization gives the characteristic equation:

λ² + (c/D)λ + (βS₀ - γ)/D = 0

For the wave to exist, this must have one positive and one negative root, requiring c² ≥ 4D(βS₀ - γ). Thus c* = 2√(D(βS₀ - γ)). □

4.1.3 6.1.3 Wave Speed Formula

Theorem 6.2 (Fisher-KPP Wave Speed). For the reaction-diffusion SIR system in the limit of small initial infection, the minimum wave speed is:

c* = 2√(D(R₀ - 1)γ)

where R₀ = βS₀/γ is the basic reproduction number.

This fundamental result shows that wave speed increases with the square root of both the diffusion coefficient and the “epidemic strength” (R₀ - 1).

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

# Solve reaction-diffusion SIR system numerically
function sir_reaction_diffusion!(du, u, p, t)
    S, I, R = u[1, :], u[2, :], u[3, :]
    β, γ, D_S, D_I, D_R = p
    nx = length(S)
    dx = 1.0  # spatial grid spacing
    
    # Compute spatial derivatives using finite differences
    for i in 2:nx-1
        # Diffusion terms (second derivatives)
        d2S_dx2 = (S[i+1] - 2*S[i] + S[i-1]) / dx^2
        d2I_dx2 = (I[i+1] - 2*I[i] + I[i-1]) / dx^2
        d2R_dx2 = (R[i+1] - 2*R[i] + R[i-1]) / dx^2
        
        # Reaction terms
        infection = β * S[i] * I[i]
        recovery = γ * I[i]
        
        # Update derivatives
        du[1, i] = D_S * d2S_dx2 - infection
        du[2, i] = D_I * d2I_dx2 + infection - recovery
        du[3, i] = D_R * d2R_dx2 + recovery
    end
    
    # Boundary conditions (no-flux)
    du[:, 1] = du[:, 2]
    du[:, end] = du[:, end-1]
end

# Set up spatial grid and initial conditions
nx = 200
x = range(0, 100, length=nx)
dx = x[2] - x[1]

# Initial conditions: localized infection
S0 = 0.99 * ones(nx)
I0 = zeros(nx)
R0 = zeros(nx)

# Small infection in the center
center = nx ÷ 2
I0[center-2:center+2] .= 0.01
S0[center-2:center+2] .= 0.98

u0 = vcat(S0', I0', R0')

# Parameters: β, γ, D_S, D_I, D_R
β, γ = 0.5, 0.2
D_S, D_I, D_R = 0.1, 0.1, 0.1
params = (β, γ, D_S, D_I, D_R)

# Solve the system
tspan = (0.0, 50.0)
prob = ODEProblem(sir_reaction_diffusion!, u0, tspan, params)
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=2.0)

# Plot space-time evolution of infected population
times = sol.t
infected_profile = [sol[i][2, :] for i in 1:length(times)]
heatmap(x, times, hcat(infected_profile...)', 
        xlabel="Space", ylabel="Time", title="Spatial SIR: Infected Population",
        color=:viridis)

# Calculate theoretical wave speed
R0 = β * 0.99 / γ  # Using initial susceptible density
c_theory = 2 * sqrt(D_I * (R0 - 1) * γ)
println("Theoretical wave speed: ", c_theory)

# Measure actual wave speed from simulation
# Find the position of maximum infection at each time
max_positions = [x[argmax(infected_profile[i])] for i in 1:length(times)]
# Linear fit to get speed
using Statistics
c_measured = (max_positions[end] - max_positions[1]) / (times[end] - times[1])
println("Measured wave speed: ", c_measured)

4.2 6.2 Human Mobility Models

Real epidemic spread depends on human movement patterns, which are far more complex than simple diffusion. Two major classes of models capture different aspects of human mobility: gravity models based on population sizes and distances, and radiation models based on intervening opportunities.

4.2.1 6.2.1 Gravity Models

Gravity models assume that the flow of individuals between locations i and j is proportional to their populations and inversely related to their distance.

Definition 6.2 (Gravity Model). The gravity model for human mobility posits that the flow T_{ij} from location i to location j is:

T_{ij} = K * (N_i^{α} N_j^{β}) / d_{ij}^{γ}

where N_i, N_j are populations, d_{ij} is distance, and K, α, β, γ are parameters.

Typical parameter values are α ≈ β ≈ 1 and γ ≈ 2, though these vary by context and scale.

4.2.2 6.2.2 Radiation Models

Radiation models are based on the concept of intervening opportunities between origin and destination.

Definition 6.3 (Radiation Model). The radiation model predicts that the flow from location i to location j is:

T_{ij} = T_i * (N_i N_j) / ((N_i + s_{ij})(N_i + N_j + s_{ij}))

where T_i is the total outflow from i, and s_{ij} is the total population within distance d_{ij} from i (excluding i and j).

The radiation model has the advantage of having no free parameters, making it more predictive than gravity models.

using Distances, LinearAlgebra, Plots

# Implement gravity and radiation models for mobility flows
struct Location
    id::Int
    x::Float64
    y::Float64
    population::Int
end

function gravity_flow(origin::Location, dest::Location; K=1.0, α=1.0, β=1.0, γ=2.0)
    if origin.id == dest.id
        return 0.0
    end
    
    distance = sqrt((origin.x - dest.x)^2 + (origin.y - dest.y)^2)
    return K * (origin.population^α * dest.population^β) / distance^γ
end

function intervening_population(origin::Location, dest::Location, all_locations::Vector{Location})
    d_ij = sqrt((origin.x - dest.x)^2 + (origin.y - dest.y)^2)
    s_ij = 0
    
    for loc in all_locations
        if loc.id != origin.id && loc.id != dest.id
            d_i_loc = sqrt((origin.x - loc.x)^2 + (origin.y - loc.y)^2)
            if d_i_loc < d_ij
                s_ij += loc.population
            end
        end
    end
    return s_ij
end

function radiation_flow(origin::Location, dest::Location, all_locations::Vector{Location}, T_i::Float64)
    if origin.id == dest.id
        return 0.0
    end
    
    s_ij = intervening_population(origin, dest, all_locations)
    N_i, N_j = origin.population, dest.population
    
    return T_i * (N_i * N_j) / ((N_i + s_ij) * (N_i + N_j + s_ij))
end

# Create a synthetic spatial network of cities
Random.seed!(123)
n_cities = 20
cities = [Location(i, 100*rand(), 100*rand(), rand(1000:50000)) for i in 1:n_cities]

# Calculate mobility matrices
gravity_matrix = zeros(n_cities, n_cities)
radiation_matrix = zeros(n_cities, n_cities)

# Assume total outflow is proportional to population
total_flows = [0.01 * city.population for city in cities]  # 1% of population travels

for i in 1:n_cities
    for j in 1:n_cities
        gravity_matrix[i,j] = gravity_flow(cities[i], cities[j])
        radiation_matrix[i,j] = radiation_flow(cities[i], cities[j], cities, total_flows[i])
    end
    
    # Normalize radiation flows to match total outflow
    if sum(radiation_matrix[i,:]) > 0
        radiation_matrix[i,:] *= total_flows[i] / sum(radiation_matrix[i,:])
    end
end

# Visualize the mobility patterns
scatter([c.x for c in cities], [c.y for c in cities], 
        markersize=[sqrt(c.population/1000) for c in cities],
        title="City Locations (size ∝ √population)", 
        xlabel="X coordinate", ylabel="Y coordinate", legend=false)

# Compare gravity vs radiation predictions
flows_gravity = [gravity_matrix[i,j] for i in 1:n_cities for j in 1:n_cities if i != j]
flows_radiation = [radiation_matrix[i,j] for i in 1:n_cities for j in 1:n_cities if i != j]

scatter(flows_gravity, flows_radiation, 
        xlabel="Gravity Model Flow", ylabel="Radiation Model Flow",
        title="Gravity vs Radiation Model Predictions", 
        alpha=0.6, legend=false)
plot!([0, maximum(flows_gravity)], [0, maximum(flows_gravity)], 
      linestyle=:dash, color=:red, label="y=x")

4.3 6.3 Metapopulation Models

Metapopulation models treat the spatial domain as a collection of discrete patches connected by movement. Each patch has its own epidemic dynamics, coupled through migration between patches.

4.3.1 6.3.1 Two-Patch SIR Model

Consider two patches with populations N₁ and N₂. Let S₁(t), I₁(t), R₁(t) and S₂(t), I₂(t), R₂(t) be the compartment sizes in each patch.

The coupled system is:

dS₁/dt = -β₁S₁I₁ - m₁₂S₁ + m₂₁S₂
dI₁/dt = β₁S₁I₁ - γ₁I₁ - m₁₂I₁ + m₂₁I₂  
dR₁/dt = γ₁I₁ - m₁₂R₁ + m₂₁R₂

and similarly for patch 2, where m_{ij} is the per-capita migration rate from patch i to patch j.

Definition 6.4 (Metapopulation Basic Reproduction Number). For a metapopulation with patches i = 1, …, n, the basic reproduction number is the spectral radius of the next-generation matrix with elements:

K_{ij} = β_j (S_j^0/N_j) ∫₀^∞ φ_{ij}(τ) e^{-γ_j τ} dτ

where φ_{ij}(τ) is the probability that an individual born in patch i is in patch j at time τ.

4.3.2 6.3.2 Invasion Threshold and Spatial R₀

When patches have different transmission parameters, the spatial distribution of the epidemic depends critically on connectivity patterns.

Theorem 6.3 (Spatial Invasion Threshold). Consider a metapopulation where patch 1 has local reproduction number R₁ > 1 and all other patches have R_i < 1. The disease can invade the entire metapopulation if and only if the largest eigenvalue of the matrix M with elements:

M_{ij} = (R_i - δ_{ij}) + δ_{ij} ∑k (m{ik}/γ_i)

is positive, where δ_{ij} is the Kronecker delta.

This result shows that even patches with R_i < 1 can sustain infection if they are sufficiently connected to high-transmission patches.

using LinearAlgebra, DifferentialEquations, Plots

# Multi-patch SIR metapopulation model
function metapop_sir!(du, u, p, t)
    β, γ, migration_matrix = p
    n_patches = length(β)
    
    # Extract S, I, R for each patch
    S = u[1:n_patches]
    I = u[n_patches+1:2*n_patches]
    R = u[2*n_patches+1:3*n_patches]
    
    for i in 1:n_patches
        # Local dynamics
        infection = β[i] * S[i] * I[i]
        recovery = γ[i] * I[i]
        
        # Migration dynamics
        S_migration = sum(migration_matrix[j,i] * S[j] for j in 1:n_patches) - 
                      sum(migration_matrix[i,j] * S[i] for j in 1:n_patches)
        I_migration = sum(migration_matrix[j,i] * I[j] for j in 1:n_patches) - 
                      sum(migration_matrix[i,j] * I[i] for j in 1:n_patches)
        R_migration = sum(migration_matrix[j,i] * R[j] for j in 1:n_patches) - 
                      sum(migration_matrix[i,j] * R[i] for j in 1:n_patches)
        
        # Update derivatives
        du[i] = -infection + S_migration
        du[n_patches + i] = infection - recovery + I_migration
        du[2*n_patches + i] = recovery + R_migration
    end
end

# Set up 4-patch metapopulation
n_patches = 4
patch_pops = [10000, 8000, 12000, 6000]

# Transmission rates (patch 1 has highest transmission)
β = [0.8, 0.4, 0.5, 0.3] ./ patch_pops  # Scale by population for density dependence
γ = [0.2, 0.2, 0.2, 0.2]

# Migration matrix (row i -> column j)
migration_matrix = [
    0.0   0.01  0.005 0.002;
    0.01  0.0   0.008 0.003;
    0.005 0.008 0.0   0.01;
    0.002 0.003 0.01  0.0
]

# Initial conditions: infection starts in patch 1
S0 = [0.99, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0] .* patch_pops
I0 = [0.01, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0] .* patch_pops  
R0_init = zeros(n_patches)

u0 = vcat(S0, I0, R0_init)
params = (β, γ, migration_matrix)

# Solve the system
tspan = (0.0, 200.0)
prob = ODEProblem(metapop_sir!, u0, tspan, params)
sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=1.0)

# Extract and plot results for each patch
times = sol.t
infected_by_patch = [sol[i][n_patches+1:2*n_patches] for i in 1:length(times)]

plot(times, hcat(infected_by_patch...)', 
     label=["Patch 1" "Patch 2" "Patch 3" "Patch 4"],
     xlabel="Time", ylabel="Infected Population", 
     title="Metapopulation SIR: Infection Spread Across Patches",
     linewidth=2)

# Calculate local R0 values for comparison
R0_local = β .* patch_pops ./ γ
println("Local R₀ values: ", R0_local)

# Calculate metapopulation R0 (simplified for this example)
# This is a rough approximation - exact calculation requires the next-generation matrix
println("Patch populations: ", patch_pops)
println("Peak infections: ", [maximum(infected_by_patch[i]) for i in 1:length(times)])

4.4 6.4 Network Epidemic Models

Network models explicitly represent the contact structure as a graph where nodes are individuals and edges represent potential transmission links. This approach captures heterogeneity in contact patterns that strongly influences epidemic dynamics.

4.4.1 6.4.1 Basic Network SIR Model

Consider a network with N nodes, each in state S, I, or R. Let A be the adjacency matrix where A_{ij} = 1 if nodes i and j are connected.

The network SIR dynamics are:

dS_i/dt = -λ_i S_i
dI_i/dt = λ_i S_i - γ I_i
dR_i/dt = γ I_i

where λ_i = τ Σ_j A_{ij} I_j is the force of infection on node i, and τ is the transmission rate per edge.

4.4.2 6.4.2 Degree-Based Mean Field Approximation

For networks with degree heterogeneity, we can approximate the dynamics using degree classes.

Definition 6.5 (Degree-Based Compartments). Let S_k(t), I_k(t), R_k(t) be the densities of susceptible, infected, and recovered nodes with degree k at time t. The degree-based mean field approximation is:

dS_k/dt = -τ k Θ(t) S_k dI_k/dt = τ k Θ(t) S_k - γ I_k
dR_k/dt = γ I_k

where Θ(t) = Σ_k k P(k) I_k(t) / is the probability that a random edge connects to an infected node.

Theorem 6.4 (Network Basic Reproduction Number). For the degree-based mean field model on a network with degree distribution P(k), the basic reproduction number is:

R₀ = (τ/γ) * (/)

where and are the first and second moments of the degree distribution.

This shows that network heterogeneity (measured by /) amplifies the reproduction number compared to a homogeneous network.

4.4.3 6.4.3 Pair Approximation

The pair approximation tracks correlations between connected nodes to improve upon mean field theory.

Definition 6.6 (Pair Approximation). The pair approximation tracks the densities of node pairs: [SI] is the density of edges connecting susceptible and infected nodes, [II] is the density of edges between infected nodes, etc. The dynamics include:

d[SI]/dt = τ[SS]Θ - τ[SI] - γ[SI] d[II]/dt = 2τ[SI] - 2γ[II]

where correlations are closed using approximations like [SSI] ≈ [SS][SI]/[S].

4.4.4 6.4.4 Message Passing and Belief Propagation

For tree-like networks, exact solutions can be found using message passing algorithms.

Theorem 6.5 (Belief Propagation for Networks). On locally tree-like networks, the probability that node i is infected by time t can be computed exactly using messages h_{i→j}(t) representing the probability that i sends infection to j by time t:

dh_{i→j}/dt = τ(1 - h_{i→j}) ∏{k∈∂i} h{k→i} - γh_{i→j}

where ∂i denotes the neighbors of node i.

using Graphs, Random, Plots, StatsBase

# Network SIR simulation using Gillespie algorithm
mutable struct NetworkNode
    id::Int
    state::Symbol  # :S, :I, or :R
    neighbors::Vector{Int}
end

struct NetworkSIR
    nodes::Vector{NetworkNode}
    τ::Float64  # transmission rate per edge
    γ::Float64  # recovery rate
end

function gillespie_network_sir(network::NetworkSIR, max_time::Float64)
    t = 0.0
    times = [0.0]
    states = [copy([node.state for node in network.nodes])]
    
    while t < max_time
        # Calculate all possible events and their rates
        events = []
        
        for node in network.nodes
            if node.state == :I
                # Recovery event
                push!(events, (node.id, :recover, network.γ))
                
                # Transmission events to susceptible neighbors
                for neighbor_id in node.neighbors
                    neighbor = network.nodes[neighbor_id]
                    if neighbor.state == :S
                        push!(events, (node.id, neighbor_id, network.τ))
                    end
                end
            end
        end
        
        if isempty(events)
            break
        end
        
        # Calculate total rate and select event
        rates = [event[end] for event in events]
        total_rate = sum(rates)
        
        # Time to next event
        dt = -log(rand()) / total_rate
        t += dt
        
        # Select which event occurs
        event_idx = sample(1:length(events), Weights(rates))
        event = events[event_idx]
        
        # Execute the event
        if event[2] == :recover
            network.nodes[event[1]].state = :R
        else
            # Transmission event
            network.nodes[event[2]].state = :I
        end
        
        # Record state
        push!(times, t)
        push!(states, copy([node.state for node in network.nodes]))
    end
    
    return times, states
end

# Create a scale-free network using preferential attachment
function create_scale_free_network(n::Int, m::Int)
    g = SimpleGraph(n)
    
    # Start with a small complete graph
    for i in 1:min(m+1, n)
        for j in i+1:min(m+1, n)
            add_edge!(g, i, j)
        end
    end
    
    # Add remaining nodes with preferential attachment
    for i in (m+2):n
        # Calculate attachment probabilities based on degree
        degrees = degree(g)
        probs = degrees[1:i-1] / sum(degrees[1:i-1])
        
        # Add m edges to existing nodes
        targets = sample(1:i-1, Weights(probs), min(m, i-1), replace=false)
        for target in targets
            add_edge!(g, i, target)
        end
    end
    
    return g
end

# Set up network epidemic
Random.seed!(42)
n_nodes = 500
m_edges = 3  # Number of edges added with each new node

# Create scale-free network
graph = create_scale_free_network(n_nodes, m_edges)

# Convert to our NetworkSIR structure
nodes = [NetworkNode(i, :S, neighbors(graph, i) |> collect) for i in 1:n_nodes]

# Initially infect a few random nodes
initial_infected = sample(1:n_nodes, 10, replace=false)
for i in initial_infected
    nodes[i].state = :I
end

# Create network epidemic model
τ = 0.1   # Transmission rate per edge
γ = 0.05  # Recovery rate
network = NetworkSIR(nodes, τ, γ)

# Run simulation
times, states = gillespie_network_sir(network, 100.0)

# Process results
S_counts = [count(s -> s == :S, state) for state in states]
I_counts = [count(s -> s == :I, state) for state in states] 
R_counts = [count(s -> s == :R, state) for state in states]

# Plot epidemic curves
plot(times, S_counts, label="Susceptible", color=:blue, linewidth=2)
plot!(times, I_counts, label="Infected", color=:red, linewidth=2)
plot!(times, R_counts, label="Recovered", color=:green, linewidth=2)
xlabel!("Time")
ylabel!("Number of Individuals")
title!("Network SIR Epidemic on Scale-Free Network")

# Calculate network properties
avg_degree = mean(degree(graph))
degree_variance = var(degree(graph))
network_R0 = (τ/γ) * (degree_variance + avg_degree) / avg_degree

println("Network properties:")
println("  Nodes: $n_nodes")
println("  Edges: $(ne(graph))")
println("  Average degree: $(round(avg_degree, digits=2))")
println("  Degree variance: $(round(degree_variance, digits=2))")
println("  Network R₀: $(round(network_R0, digits=2))")
println("  Final epidemic size: $(R_counts[end]/n_nodes * 100)%")

4.4.5 6.4.5 Percolation and Epidemic Thresholds

There is a deep connection between epidemic spread on networks and percolation theory.

Definition 6.7 (Bond Percolation). In bond percolation, each edge is independently removed with probability 1-p. The percolation threshold p_c is the critical probability above which a giant connected component exists.

Theorem 6.6 (Epidemic-Percolation Correspondence). For SIR epidemics on networks, the final epidemic size equals the size of the connected component in bond percolation where each edge transmits infection with probability T = τ/(τ+γ).

The epidemic threshold occurs when T = T_c, where T_c is the percolation threshold.

Theorem 6.7 (Percolation Threshold for Random Networks). For configuration model networks with degree distribution P(k), the percolation threshold is:

T_c = /( - )

The corresponding epidemic threshold is R₀ = (τ/γ)(/) = 1.

4.5 6.5 Spatial Heterogeneity and Control

Spatial structure creates opportunities for targeted interventions that can be more effective than uniform population-wide measures.

4.5.1 6.5.1 Ring Vaccination

Ring vaccination targets the spatial neighborhood around detected cases.

Definition 6.8 (Ring Vaccination Strategy). Upon detection of infection at location x, vaccinate all susceptible individuals within distance r of x at rate ν.

4.5.2 6.5.2 Spatial Quarantine

Travel restrictions can slow spatial spread by reducing connectivity between regions.

Definition 6.9 (Spatial Quarantine). Reduce migration rates between regions by a factor (1-q) where q ∈ [0,1] is the quarantine effectiveness.

# Example: Effect of spatial quarantine on metapopulation outbreak
function metapop_sir_quarantine!(du, u, p, t)
    β, γ, migration_matrix, quarantine_start, quarantine_effectiveness = p
    n_patches = length(β)
    
    # Apply quarantine after quarantine_start time
    effective_migration = if t >= quarantine_start
        (1 - quarantine_effectiveness) * migration_matrix
    else
        migration_matrix
    end
    
    # Same dynamics as before but with modified migration
    S = u[1:n_patches]
    I = u[n_patches+1:2*n_patches]
    R = u[2*n_patches+1:3*n_patches]
    
    for i in 1:n_patches
        infection = β[i] * S[i] * I[i]
        recovery = γ[i] * I[i]
        
        S_migration = sum(effective_migration[j,i] * S[j] for j in 1:n_patches)


\newpage

# Chapter 7: Parameter Estimation and Model Fitting

The compartmental epidemic models developed in previous chapters contain parameters—transmission rates, recovery rates, population mixing patterns—that must be estimated from observed data to make the models predictive and useful for public health decision making. This chapter develops the statistical theory and computational methods for parameter estimation in epidemic models, covering maximum likelihood estimation for deterministic models, Bayesian inference for both deterministic and stochastic systems, and specialized techniques for real-time epidemic monitoring.

Unlike parameter estimation in standard statistical models, epidemic parameter estimation presents unique challenges: observations are typically partial (we observe incidence, not the full state), the underlying dynamics are nonlinear, parameters may be time-varying, and model uncertainty is substantial. We address these challenges through a progression from classical maximum likelihood methods to modern Bayesian computational techniques.

## Maximum Likelihood Estimation for Epidemic Data

### The Likelihood for Incidence Data

Consider a deterministic SIR model with parameters θ = (β, γ) governing transmission and recovery rates. Let S(t; θ), I(t; θ), R(t; θ) denote the solution with initial conditions S(0), I(0), R(0). We observe new infections (incidence) at discrete time points: y₁, y₂, ..., yₙ at times t₁, t₂, ..., tₙ.

The observed incidence yᵢ represents new infections occurring in the interval [tᵢ₋₁, tᵢ]. For the SIR model, the rate of new infections at time t is βS(t)I(t)/N, so the expected number of new infections in [tᵢ₋₁, tᵢ] is:

λᵢ(θ) = ∫[tᵢ₋₁ to tᵢ] β S(s; θ)I(s; θ)/N ds

> **Definition 7.1 (Poisson Incidence Likelihood).** If new infections follow a Poisson process with time-varying rate β S(t)I(t)/N, then the likelihood for observed incidence data y = (y₁, ..., yₙ) is:
> 
> L(θ; y) = ∏ᵢ₌₁ⁿ [λᵢ(θ)]^yᵢ exp(-λᵢ(θ)) / yᵢ!

The log-likelihood is:

ℓ(θ; y) = Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ [yᵢ log λᵢ(θ) - λᵢ(θ) - log yᵢ!]

Computing λᵢ(θ) requires solving the ODE system numerically and then evaluating the integral, which can be approximated using the trapezoidal rule or higher-order quadrature methods.

### Maximum Likelihood Implementation

```julia
using DifferentialEquations, Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL, ForwardDiff

function sir_model!(du, u, p, t)
    S, I, R = u
    β, γ = p
    N = S + I + R
    
    du[1] = -β * S * I / N  # dS/dt
    du[2] = β * S * I / N - γ * I  # dI/dt
    du[3] = γ * I  # dR/dt
end

function compute_incidence_integral(sol, β, t_start, t_end; N=1.0)
    """Compute expected incidence in interval [t_start, t_end]"""
    function incidence_rate(t)
        S_t, I_t, _ = sol(t)
        return β * S_t * I_t / N
    end
    
    # Simple trapezoidal rule integration
    n_steps = 10
    dt = (t_end - t_start) / n_steps
    integral = 0.0
    
    for i in 0:n_steps-1
        t1 = t_start + i * dt
        t2 = t_start + (i + 1) * dt
        integral += 0.5 * dt * (incidence_rate(t1) + incidence_rate(t2))
    end
    
    return integral
end

function sir_log_likelihood(params, data)
    """Compute log-likelihood for SIR model given incidence data"""
    β, γ = params
    times, incidence = data
    
    if β <= 0 || γ <= 0
        return -Inf
    end
    
    # Initial conditions (assume known for simplicity)
    N = 1000.0
    I0 = 1.0
    S0 = N - I0
    R0 = 0.0
    
    u0 = [S0, I0, R0]
    tspan = (0.0, maximum(times))
    prob = ODEProblem(sir_model!, u0, (β, γ), tspan)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=times)
    
    if sol.retcode != :Success
        return -Inf
    end
    
    log_likelihood = 0.0
    
    for i in 2:length(times)
        # Expected incidence in interval [times[i-1], times[i]]
        λ = compute_incidence_integral(sol, β, times[i-1], times[i], N=N)
        
        if λ <= 0
            return -Inf
        end
        
        # Poisson log-likelihood (omitting constant terms)
        y = incidence[i]
        log_likelihood += y * log(λ) - λ
    end
    
    return log_likelihood
end

# Example: Maximum likelihood estimation
function fit_sir_mle(times, incidence)
    """Fit SIR model to incidence data using maximum likelihood"""
    
    # Objective function (negative log-likelihood)
    objective(params, data) = -sir_log_likelihood(params, data)
    
    # Initial parameter guess
    p0 = [0.5, 0.1]  # [β, γ]
    
    # Set up optimization problem
    optf = OptimizationFunction(objective, Optimization.AutoForwardDiff())
    prob = OptimizationProblem(optf, p0, (times, incidence),
                             lb=[0.01, 0.01], ub=[5.0, 2.0])
    
    # Solve optimization
    sol = solve(prob, BFGS())
    
    return sol.u, sol.minimum
end

# Generate synthetic data for demonstration
true_β, true_γ = 0.8, 0.2
times = 0.0:1.0:30.0
N = 1000.0

# Solve true model
u0 = [N-1, 1.0, 0.0]
prob_true = ODEProblem(sir_model!, u0, (true_β, true_γ), (0.0, 30.0))
sol_true = solve(prob_true, Tsit5(), saveat=times)

# Generate synthetic incidence data with Poisson noise
incidence = zeros(Int, length(times))
for i in 2:length(times)
    λ = compute_incidence_integral(sol_true, true_β, times[i-1], times[i], N=N)
    incidence[i] = rand(Poisson(λ))
end

# Fit model
β_hat, γ_hat = fit_sir_mle(times, incidence)[1]
println("True parameters: β = $true_β, γ = $true_γ")
println("Estimated parameters: β = $(round(β_hat, digits=3)), γ = $(round(γ_hat, digits=3))")

4.5.3 Confidence Intervals and Asymptotic Theory

Theorem 7.1 (Asymptotic Normality of MLE). Under regularity conditions, as the sample size n → ∞, the maximum likelihood estimator θ̂ₙ satisfies:

√n(θ̂ₙ - θ₀) → N(0, I(θ₀)⁻¹)

where I(θ₀) is the Fisher information matrix.

For epidemic models, the Fisher information matrix can be computed numerically:

I(θ) = -E[∇²ℓ(θ; Y)] = -E[∂²ℓ(θ; Y)/∂θᵢ∂θⱼ]

In practice, we approximate this using the observed information:

Î(θ̂) = -∇²ℓ(θ̂; y)

using LinearAlgebra

function compute_confidence_intervals(params, data; α=0.05)
    """Compute asymptotic confidence intervals for MLE"""
    
    # Compute observed information matrix (Hessian of negative log-likelihood)
    objective(p) = -sir_log_likelihood(p, data)
    hessian = ForwardDiff.hessian(objective, params)
    
    # Fisher information is negative Hessian of log-likelihood
    fisher_info = -hessian
    
    # Covariance matrix is inverse of Fisher information
    if det(fisher_info) > 1e-10
        cov_matrix = inv(fisher_info)
        standard_errors = sqrt.(diag(cov_matrix))
        
        # Normal approximation confidence intervals
        z_critical = quantile(Normal(), 1 - α/2)
        ci_lower = params - z_critical * standard_errors
        ci_upper = params + z_critical * standard_errors
        
        return ci_lower, ci_upper, standard_errors
    else
        @warn "Fisher information matrix is singular"
        return nothing, nothing, nothing
    end
end

4.6 Bayesian Inference for Compartmental Models

4.6.1 Prior Specification and ODE-Based Likelihoods

Bayesian inference treats parameters as random variables with prior distributions. For epidemic parameters, common choices include:

  • Transmission rate: β ~ Gamma(aβ, bβ) (ensures β > 0)
  • Recovery rate: γ ~ Gamma(aγ, bγ) (ensures γ > 0)
  • Initial conditions: I(0) ~ Poisson(λI) or Uniform(1, Imax)

The posterior distribution combines the prior with the likelihood:

p(θ | y) ∝ p(y | θ) p(θ)

using Turing, Distributions

@model function sir_bayesian(incidence, times, N)
    # Priors
    β ~ Gamma(2.0, 1/0.5)  # Mean 1.0, std ~0.7
    γ ~ Gamma(2.0, 1/0.2)  # Mean 0.4, std ~0.28
    I0 ~ DiscreteUniform(1, 10)
    
    # Initial conditions
    S0 = N - I0
    R0 = 0.0
    
    # Solve ODE
    u0 = [S0, I0, R0]
    tspan = (0.0, maximum(times))
    prob = ODEProblem(sir_model!, u0, (β, γ), tspan)
    
    # Handle potential ODE solver failures
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=times, verbose=false)
    
    if sol.retcode != :Success
        Turing.@addlogprob! -Inf
        return
    end
    
    # Likelihood for each time interval
    for i in 2:length(times)
        λ = compute_incidence_integral(sol, β, times[i-1], times[i], N=N)
        if λ <= 0
            Turing.@addlogprob! -Inf
            return
        end
        incidence[i] ~ Poisson(λ)
    end
end

# Fit Bayesian model
function fit_sir_bayesian(times, incidence, N; n_samples=2000, n_chains=4)
    model = sir_bayesian(incidence, times, N)
    
    # Use NUTS sampler
    chain = sample(model, NUTS(0.65), MCMCSerial(), n_samples, n_chains)
    
    return chain
end

# Example usage (continuing from previous synthetic data)
chain = fit_sir_bayesian(times, incidence, N, n_samples=1000, n_chains=2)
println(chain)

4.6.2 MCMC Challenges in Epidemic Models

Epidemic models present several challenges for MCMC sampling:

  1. High correlation between β and γ when R₀ = β/γ is well-identified but individual parameters are not
  2. Multimodality in parameter space due to model non-identifiability
  3. Computational cost of solving ODEs at each iteration
  4. Stiff dynamics when some populations become very small

Definition 7.2 (Effective Sample Size). For an MCMC chain {θ(t)}ᵀₜ₌₁, the effective sample size for parameter θᵢ is:

ESS(θᵢ) = T / (1 + 2 Σₖ₌₁^∞ ρₖ(θᵢ))

where ρₖ(θᵢ) is the autocorrelation at lag k.

# Analyze MCMC diagnostics
using MCMCDiagnosticTools, StatsPlots

function analyze_mcmc_diagnostics(chain)
    # Effective sample sizes
    ess_values = ess_rhat(chain)
    println("Effective Sample Sizes:")
    println(ess_values.ess)
    
    # R-hat values (should be close to 1.0)
    println("\nR-hat values:")
    println(ess_values.rhat)
    
    # Trace plots
    p1 = plot(chain[:β], title="β trace")
    p2 = plot(chain[:γ], title="γ trace")
    p3 = plot(chain[:I0], title="I0 trace")
    
    return plot(p1, p2, p3, layout=(3,1), size=(800,600))
end

# Check for convergence issues
diagnostic_plots = analyze_mcmc_diagnostics(chain)

4.6.3 Reparameterization Strategies

To improve MCMC efficiency, we can reparameterize using epidemiologically meaningful quantities:

@model function sir_bayesian_reparam(incidence, times, N)
    # Reparameterization: use R₀ and γ instead of β and γ
    R0 ~ Gamma(2.5, 1/1.5)  # Mean 3.75, focused on plausible R₀ values
    γ ~ Gamma(5.0, 1/0.2)   # Mean 1.0 (recovery rate)
    I0 ~ DiscreteUniform(1, 10)
    
    # Derive β from R₀ and γ
    β = R0 * γ
    
    # Rest of the model unchanged
    S0 = N - I0
    R0_init = 0.0
    
    u0 = [S0, I0, R0_init]
    tspan = (0.0, maximum(times))
    prob = ODEProblem(sir_model!, u0, (β, γ), tspan)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=times, verbose=false)
    
    if sol.retcode != :Success
        Turing.@addlogprob! -Inf
        return
    end
    
    for i in 2:length(times)
        λ = compute_incidence_integral(sol, β, times[i-1], times[i], N=N)
        if λ <= 0
            Turing.@addlogprob! -Inf
            return
        end
        incidence[i] ~ Poisson(λ)
    end
end

4.7 Approximate Bayesian Computation for Stochastic Models

When the likelihood is intractable—as in stochastic epidemic models—Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) provides an alternative approach. ABC approximates the posterior by simulation: we accept parameter values that produce simulated data “close” to the observed data.

Definition 7.3 (ABC Algorithm). Given observed data y₀, tolerance ε > 0, and distance function d(·,·), the ABC posterior approximation is:

p_ABC(θ | y₀) ∝ p(θ) ∫ 𝟙{d(y, y₀) ≤ ε} p(y | θ) dy

where 𝟙{·} is the indicator function.

For epidemic models, common distance functions include: - Euclidean distance between incidence curves - Wasserstein distance between epidemic trajectories
- Distance between summary statistics (peak timing, final size, etc.)

using Random, StatsBase

function simulate_stochastic_sir(β, γ, N, I0, tmax; dt=0.1)
    """Simulate stochastic SIR using tau-leaping approximation"""
    times = 0.0:dt:tmax
    S, I, R = N - I0, I0, 0
    
    trajectory = Matrix{Int}(undef, length(times), 3)
    incidence = zeros(Int, length(times))
    
    for (i, t) in enumerate(times)
        trajectory[i, :] = [S, I, R]
        
        if i > 1
            # Record new infections in this time step
            prev_R = trajectory[i-1, 3]
            incidence[i] = R - prev_R
        end
        
        if I == 0
            # Epidemic extinct
            trajectory[i:end, :] .= [S, 0, R]
            break
        end
        
        # Tau-leaping: Poisson approximation for small dt
        infection_rate = β * S * I / N
        recovery_rate = γ * I
        
        new_infections = min(S, rand(Poisson(infection_rate * dt)))
        new_recoveries = min(I, rand(Poisson(recovery_rate * dt)))
        
        S -= new_infections
        I = I + new_infections - new_recoveries
        R += new_recoveries
    end
    
    return times, trajectory, incidence
end

function epidemic_summary_stats(incidence, times)
    """Compute summary statistics for ABC distance"""
    total_infections = sum(incidence)
    
    if total_infections == 0
        return [0.0, 0.0, 0.0, 0.0]
    end
    
    # Peak day
    peak_day = times[argmax(incidence)]
    
    # Peak incidence
    peak_incidence = maximum(incidence)
    
    # Time to 90% of final size
    cumulative = cumsum(incidence)
    ninety_percent = 0.9 * total_infections
    t90_idx = findfirst(x -> x >= ninety_percent, cumulative)
    t90 = t90_idx === nothing ? maximum(times) : times[t90_idx]
    
    return [total_infections, peak_day, peak_incidence, t90]
end

function abc_sir_inference(observed_incidence, times; 
                          n_samples=10000, tolerance=0.1)
    """ABC inference for stochastic SIR model"""
    
    N = 1000
    tmax = maximum(times)
    observed_stats = epidemic_summary_stats(observed_incidence, times)
    
    accepted_params = Vector{Vector{Float64}}()
    distances = Float64[]
    
    # Priors
    β_prior = Gamma(2.0, 1/0.5)
    γ_prior = Gamma(2.0, 1/0.2)
    I0_prior = DiscreteUniform(1, 10)
    
    for i in 1:n_samples
        # Sample from priors
        β = rand(β_prior)
        γ = rand(γ_prior)
        I0 = rand(I0_prior)
        
        # Simulate model
        _, _, sim_incidence = simulate_stochastic_sir(β, γ, N, I0, tmax)
        
        # Interpolate to observed time points
        sim_incidence_interp = [sim_incidence[findfirst(t -> t >= time, collect(0.0:0.1:tmax))] 
                               for time in times]
        
        # Compute summary statistics
        sim_stats = epidemic_summary_stats(sim_incidence_interp, times)
        
        # Compute distance
        dist = norm(sim_stats - observed_stats)
        push!(distances, dist)
        
        # Accept if within tolerance
        if dist <= tolerance
            push!(accepted_params, [β, γ, I0])
        end
        
        if i % 1000 == 0
            acceptance_rate = length(accepted_params) / i
            println("Sample $i: acceptance rate = $(round(acceptance_rate, digits=3))")
        end
    end
    
    if isempty(accepted_params)
        @warn "No parameters accepted. Consider increasing tolerance."
        # Return best fitting parameters
        best_idx = argmin(distances)
        return [accepted_params], minimum(distances)
    end
    
    return accepted_params, minimum(distances)
end

# Example ABC inference
abc_results, min_distance = abc_sir_inference(incidence, times, 
                                            n_samples=5000, tolerance=50.0)

if !isempty(abc_results)
    β_samples = [p[1] for p in abc_results]
    γ_samples = [p[2] for p in abc_results]
    
    println("ABC Results:")
    println("β: mean = $(round(mean(β_samples), digits=3)), std = $(round(std(β_samples), digits=3))")
    println("γ: mean = $(round(mean(γ_samples), digits=3)), std = $(round(std(γ_samples), digits=3))")
    println("Number of accepted samples: $(length(abc_results))")
end

4.8 Particle MCMC Methods

Particle MCMC combines sequential Monte Carlo (particle filtering) with MCMC to enable Bayesian inference for state-space models where the likelihood is intractable but simulation is feasible.

Definition 7.4 (Particle Marginal Metropolis-Hastings). The PMMH algorithm uses a particle filter to estimate the likelihood p̂(y | θ) with N particles, then accepts/rejects θ proposals based on:

α = min(1, [p̂(y | θ) p(θ)] / [p̂(y | θ) p(θ)] × [q(θ | θ*)] / [q(θ* | θ)])

The key insight is that the particle filter provides an unbiased estimate of the likelihood, making the PMMH algorithm exact asymptotically.

function particle_filter_sir(β, γ, N, I0, observations, times; n_particles=1000)
    """Particle filter for stochastic SIR model"""
    
    dt = times[2] - times[1]  # Assume regular time spacing
    n_times = length(times)
    
    # Initialize particles
    particles = [(N - I0, I0, 0) for _ in 1:n_particles]  # (S, I, R)
    weights = fill(1.0/n_particles, n_particles)
    log_likelihood = 0.0
    
    for t_idx in 2:n_times
        # Prediction step: advance particles
        new_particles = Vector{Tuple{Int,Int,Int}}(undef, n_particles)
        
        for p in 1:n_particles
            S, I, R = particles[p]
            
            if I > 0
                # Stochastic transitions
                infection_rate = β * S * I / N
                recovery_rate = γ * I
                
                new_infections = min(S, rand(Poisson(infection_rate * dt)))
                new_recoveries = min(I, rand(Poisson(recovery_rate * dt)))
                
                S_new = S - new_infections
                I_new = I + new_infections - new_recoveries
                R_new = R + new_recoveries
                
                new_particles[p] = (S_new, I_new, R_new)
            else
                # Epidemic extinct
                new_particles[p] = (S, 0, R)
            end
        end
        
        particles = new_particles
        
        # Update step: weight particles based on observation
        if !ismissing(observations[t_idx])
            # Observation model: new recoveries ~ observed incidence
            obs = observations[t_idx]
            
            for p in 1:n_particles
                prev_R = p > 1 ? particles[p][3] - (new_particles[p][3] - particles[p][3]) : 0
                new_infections_p = particles[p][3] - prev_R
                
                # Poisson observation likelihood
                if new_infections_p >= 0
                    weights[p] *= pdf(Poisson(new_infections_p + 1e-6), obs)
                else
                    weights[p] = 0.0
                end
            end
            
            # Normalize weights
            total_weight = sum(weights)
            if total_weight > 0
                weights ./= total_weight
                log_likelihood += log(total_weight)
            else
                return -Inf  # All particles have zero weight
            end
            
            # Resample if effective sample size is low
            ess = 1.0 / sum(weights.^2)
            if ess < n_particles / 2
                # Systematic resampling
                indices = systematic_resample(weights)
                particles = particles[indices]
                weights = fill(1.0/n_particles, n_particles)
            end
        end
    end
    
    return log_likelihood
end

function systematic_resample(weights)
    """Systematic resampling for particle filter"""
    n = length(weights)
    cumsum_weights = cumsum(weights)
    indices = Vector{Int}(undef, n)
    
    u = rand() / n
    for i in 1:n
        target = (i - 1 + u) / n
        indices[i] = findfirst(x -> x >= target, cumsum_weights)
    end
    
    return indices
end

# Particle MCMC implementation would go here...
# (Full implementation is quite involved and would exceed space limits)

4.9 Iterated Filtering Methods

Iterated filtering (IF2) is a method for maximum likelihood estimation in partially observed stochastic dynamical systems. The algorithm alternates between filtering (estimating the unobserved state) and parameter perturbation.

Definition 7.5 (IF2 Algorithm). At iteration m, IF2 performs the following steps: 1. Perturb parameters: θₚ ~ N(θₘ₋₁, σₘ²Σ) 2. Run particle filter with perturbed parameters 3. Update parameter estimates using filtering results 4. Cool perturbation variance: σₘ₊₁ = ρσₘ

The method converges to maximum likelihood estimates under regularity conditions.

4.10 Real-Time Estimation of Rt

The instantaneous reproduction number Rt measures the expected number of secondary infections from a primary infection at time t. Real-time estimation of Rt is crucial for epidemic monitoring and control decisions.

4.10.1 The EpiEstim Approach

The EpiEstim method (Cori et al., 2013) estimates Rt using the renewal equation:

Iₜ = Rₜ Σₛ₌₁ᵗ Iₜ₋ₛ wₛ

where Iₜ is incidence at time t and wₛ is the generation time distribution.

Definition 7.6 (Renewal Equation Method). Assuming a Poisson likelihood for incidence and a Gamma prior for Rt, the posterior for Rt given observations up to time t is:

Rt | I₁:t ~ Gamma(a + Σₛ₌₁ᵗ Iₛ, b + Σₛ₌₁ᵗ Λₛ)

where Λₛ = Σᵤ₌₁ˢ Iₛ₋ᵤ wᵤ is the total infectiousness at time s.

using Distributions

function estimate_rt_epiestim(incidence, generation_time_pmf; 
                             window_size=7, prior_a=1.0, prior_b=5.0)
    """Estimate Rt using EpiEstim method"""
    
    n_times = length(incidence)
    rt_estimates = Vector{Float64}(undef, n_times)
    rt_lower = Vector{Float64}(undef, n_times)
    rt_upper = Vector{Float64}(undef, n_times)
    
    # Pad generation time PMF if needed
    max_gen_time = min(length(generation_time_pmf), n_times)
    w = generation_time_pmf[1:max_gen_time]
    
    for t in window_size:n_times
        # Time window for estimation
        window_start = max(1, t - window_size + 1)
        
        # Compute total infectiousness (Lambda) for each time in window
        total_incidence = 0.0
        total_infectiousness = 0.0
        
        for s in window_start:t
            if s > length(incidence)
                break
            end
            
            total_incidence += incidence[s]
            
            # Compute infectiousness at time s
            lambda_s = 0.0
            for tau in 1:min(s-1, length(w))
                if s - tau >= 1
                    lambda_s += incidence[s - tau] * w[tau]
                end
            end
            total_infectiousness += lambda_s
        end
        
        if total_infectiousness > 0
            # Posterior parameters for Gamma distribution
            post_a = prior_a + total_incidence
            post_b = prior_b + total_infectiousness
            
            # Posterior mean and credible interval
            rt_estimates[t] = post_a / post_b
            
            # 95% credible interval
            posterior = Gamma(post_a, 1/post_b)
            rt_lower[t] = quantile(posterior, 0.025)
            rt_upper[t] = quantile(posterior, 0.975)
        else
            # No infectiousness data available
            rt_estimates[t] = prior_a / prior_b
            rt_lower[t] = 0.0
            rt_upper[t] = 10.0  # Very wide interval
        end
    end
    
    # Fill in early values (before window_size)
    for t in 1:(window_size-1)
        rt_estimates[t] = rt_estimates[window_size]
        rt_lower[t] = rt_lower[window_size]
        rt_upper[t] = rt_upper[window_size]
    end
    
    return rt_estimates, rt_lower, rt_upper
end

# Example: Estimate Rt for synthetic epidemic data
function example_rt_estimation()
    # Generate generation time distribution (Gamma with mean 5, std 2)
    max_gen_time = 20
    gen_time_dist = Gamma(6.25, 0.8)  # shape=6.25, rate=1.25 gives mean=5
    generation_time_pmf = [pdf(gen_time_dist, t) for t in 1:max_gen_time]
    generation_time_pmf ./= sum(generation_time_pmf)  # Normalize
    
    # Use our synthetic incidence data
    rt_est, rt_lower,


\newpage

# Chapter 8: Optimal Control of Epidemics

The control of infectious disease outbreaks requires careful allocation of limited resources across time, space, and populations. From vaccination campaigns to antiviral distribution, public health authorities face complex optimization problems where the stakes are measured in lives saved and economic costs avoided. This chapter develops the mathematical framework for optimal epidemic control, connecting classical control theory with compartmental disease models to provide principled approaches to intervention design.

## 8.1 Formulation of Epidemic Control Problems

### 8.1.1 The General Control Framework

Consider a compartmental epidemic model with state variables **x**(t) ∈ ℝⁿ and control variables **u**(t) ∈ U ⊆ ℝᵐ. The controlled epidemic system takes the form:

dx/dt = f(x(t), u(t), t), x(0) = x₀
